understanding.goal_understanding: The goal of the game was to complete each level by arranging or moving pieces so that a required condition was satisfied, typically getting at least one pushable object onto a goal tile marked with '+'. When that condition was met, the engine reported WIN for the level (and for the overall run once the final level was cleared). The run ended unsuccessfully here because the remaining steps were exhausted before achieving the level’s win condition.
understanding.mechanics_understanding: Movement was grid-based with a currently selected piece. Arrows (MOVE_LEFT, MOVE_RIGHT, MOVE_UP, MOVE_DOWN) attempted to move that selected piece one or more cells in the indicated direction, subject to obstacles and rules. If the way was blocked by a wall or an unmovable configuration, the position did not change and the step was still consumed. Some objects could be pushed: when the selected piece moved into a cell containing a pushable object and the next cell in that direction was free, both would shift, allowing indirect repositioning. Walls or other solid obstacles prevented such motion. Selection worked via CLICK: clicking a coordinate teleported or transferred the ‘selected’ status to a different piece, effectively switching control from one piece to another, sometimes by moving the previously selected piece’s presence to the clicked location. Each action (move or click) reduced the steps remaining, and once steps reached zero, the run ended regardless of progress.

Agent explained goal and mechanics.

understanding.goal_understanding: The goal of the game was to complete each level by arranging or moving pieces so that a required condition was satisfied, typically getting at least one pushable object onto a goal tile marked with '+'. When that condition was met, the engine reported WIN for the level (and for the overall run once the final level was cleared). The run ended unsuccessfully here because the remaining steps were exhausted before achieving the level’s win condition.
understanding.mechanics_understanding: Movement was grid-based with a currently selected piece. Arrows (MOVE_LEFT, MOVE_RIGHT, MOVE_UP, MOVE_DOWN) attempted to move that selected piece one or more cells in the indicated direction, subject to obstacles and rules. If the way was blocked by a wall or an unmovable configuration, the position did not change and the step was still consumed. Some objects could be pushed: when the selected piece moved into a cell containing a pushable object and the next cell in that direction was free, both would shift, allowing indirect repositioning. Walls or other solid obstacles prevented such motion. Selection worked via CLICK: clicking a coordinate teleported or transferred the ‘selected’ status to a different piece, effectively switching control from one piece to another, sometimes by moving the previously selected piece’s presence to the clicked location. Each action (move or click) reduced the steps remaining, and once steps reached zero, the run ended regardless of progress.
